data.name: Mert Günok
data.description: There was perhaps never any doubt about which position Mert Gunok would play in as his father was a former goalkeeper and then a goalkeeping coach. Despite this Mert nearly chose a career in basketball before football. He is 37 now but despite making his international debut in 2012, he had not reached 40 caps by the time this World Cup started, being in and out of the side. He was first choice, however, as Turkey reached the Euro 2024 quarter-finals, with his fingertip save against Austria one of the key moments of his country’s campaign. With Turkey leading 2-1 and the game deep into added-on time Christoph Baumgartner steered a powerful header into the ground and towards the Turkey goal but Gunok somehow launched himself to his right to push the ball beyond the post. “It was one of the best moments of my life,” he said. “I have a photo of the save which I will frame and put up on my wall.”

data.name: Zeki Çelik
data.description: The youngest of nine brothers, he was raised in Bursa but has spent most of his professional career in Ligue 1 (Lille) and Serie A (Roma). He is a self-confessed football obsessive who once admitted that he spent 45 consecutive days during the 2020 lockdown playing football video games to maintain his tactical sharpness. "I sleep thinking about football and I wake up thinking about football," he has said. One of the most consistent and reliable players in the team and he is set to be a certain starter in North America. Having arrived at Roma when José Mourinho was the manager, Celik told Il Romanista what he made of the Portuguese: "Seen from the outside he gives the impression of being a character, and … basically he is. Seen from the inside he is someone who treats players very well and who knows how to make them love him."

data.name: Merih Demiral
data.description: Demiral has rediscovered his form and confidence after a rocky period, which included an anterior cruciate ligament injury while at Juventus in January 2020. After a spell at Atalanta he now appears settled at Al-Ahli. The 1.90m tall defender is a natural leader; the type of centre-back who celebrates a successful sliding tackle as if he has scored the most important goal ever. The 28-year-old also poses a threat from set pieces going forwards and scored both goals in the Euro 2024 last-16 tie against Austria, the first one coming after 57 seconds. He has been involved in controversy too; first in 2019 as he was one of several Turkish players making a so-called “military salute” goal celebration against Albania, and then for making a “wolf salute” after the win against Austria. Demiral was unrepentant in face of criticism and vowed to do it again, while the incident led to Turkey’s ambassador to Germany being summoned to the foreign ministry in Berlin to explain the gesture and to take measures to prevent its further use. Demiral was banned for two matches by Uefa.

data.name: Çağlar Söyüncü
data.description: Not long ago the man with the most recognisable jawline in football - and a distinctive haircut to go with it – was a guaranteed starter at the heart of the Turkish defence, but these days he is rarely picked by Vincenzo Montella. In fact, he did not even make the squad for the World Cup play-off games against Romania and Kosovo. Was probably at the peak of his career at Leicester, between 2018 and 2023, winning the FA Cup. He then joined Atlético Madrid and is now back in Turkey with Fenerbahce. In 2022 he told the Times about leaving home as an 11-year-old to board and play at Bucaspor, a club on the outskirts of Izmir. “Leaving home when I was 11 was very painful,” he said. “Tears? Yes, at night. Some players would go back to their homes after training but there were about 25 of us there, staying in like a dormitory, working really hard, cleaning toilets, sweeping the floor, doing washing up in order to learn about life. The lights would go off at 9pm. We were like orphans.”

data.name: Salih Özcan
data.description: A quirky detail: Ozcan is a highly skilled amateur wrestler, which is befitting the style of football he plays. A disciplined and combative midfielder with years of Bundesliga experience, he grew up in Cologne and represented Germany at youth levels before switching to Turkey, driven by a desire to represent his heritage. Known for his professionalism, he is often the first player on the training pitch and the last to leave. Nicknamed ‘The cleaner’ in Germany, he made almost 130 appearances for Cologne before the switch to Borussia Dortmund in 2025. Talking to the BVB website about why enjoys wrestling so much he said: “Probably that it's completely different from football. In wrestling, you use your entire body; from head to toe, you have to be tense every second. And there are always new techniques you hadn't even thought of before. Once you start, you always want to keep going, always want to learn more.”

data.name: Orkun Kökçü
data.description: Kokcu made the surprise move from Benfica to Besiktas this summer, saying: "Finally, I am playing for the team of my childhood dreams." And he has excelled in Istanbul. The midfield dynamo is one of the unsung heroes of his team: a lot of what he brings to the table goes unnoticed but he puts in a lot of hard work and keeps things ticking over. Like Kadioglu he was born in the Netherlands and, as with Kadioglu, expect him to make a big impact in North America. Things did not go according to plan at Benfica after a £26m move from Feyenoord in 2023 and it all came to a head with an almighty falling-out with the coach, Bruno Lage, at the Club World Cup in 2025. Benfica were winning 2-0 against Auckland when Lage took off the No 10, which led to a row between the two and ended with the manager shushing his own player.

data.name: Kerem Aktürkoğlu
data.description: Known to fans as Harry Potter for his signature goal celebration, waving an imaginary magic wand; Akturkoglu is one of few footballers to have played for both Galatasaray and Fenerbahce. The winger wants to demonstrate magic of a different kind this summer: "I want to show the world that Turkish players have magic in their feet." Has been playing up front for Turkey despite being a traditional winger as Vincenzo Montella has lacked options at No 9 because of injuries but his place in the side is not guaranteed: it is the one position that is still up for grabs.

data.name: Arda Güler
data.description: “The last time we were able to go to the World Cup, I didn’t even exist,” Guler said after Turkey had qualified for the tournament for the first time since 2002 – and the Real Madrid man is the undisputed face of this side. After proving himself at one of the biggest clubs in the world and shining for the national team he is now preparing to take centre stage in his biggest test yet. “We are talking about an undisputed, elite world-class talent,” is how Thierry Henry recently described the Turkish attacking midfielder. The 21-year-old may have a babyface but he is no pushover and not a luxury player. The midfielder was involved in a staggering 44% of the Red-Whites goals in games he played in for the 2025-26 qualifying campaign and he showed his passion and precociousness in the Champions League quarter-final away leg against Bayern. First he scored an outrageous long-range goal in the first minute, then a second within half an hour before getting sent off in added time for dissent.

data.name: Deniz Gül
data.description: Born in Stockholm and raised in the Hammarby youth system, Gul was a staple of Sweden’s youth setups until a late-night phone call from Vincenzo Montella in late 2024 changed his trajectory. He is a modern, mobile No 9 with a 1.90m (6ft 3in) frame and on paper just what the national team have been missing up front. Since moving to Porto for a mere €4.5m, his development has accelerated. "I followed my heart; representing Turkey is a dream for my family," he said upon receiving his first senior call-up. He has yet to cement his place with the national team but his is the position that remains up for grabs heading into the World Cup.

data.name: Hakan Çalhanoğlu
data.description: Never one to shy away from self-adulation, Calhanoglu has a reputation as being a free-kick master – despite not having a great scoring record from set pieces – and in 2024 described himself as “the best deep-lying playmaker in the world right now”. Rodri was second on Calhanoglu’s list and Toni Kroos third. "I may be further away from the box now," he says of his more defensive role, "but I have greater control over the game." To be fair, he was instrumental as Inter reached the Champions League final in 2025 and, although he has never quite replicated his club form at international level, the fact that the spotlight will be on Arda Guler and Kenan Yildiz this summer may have a liberating effect on him.

data.name: Kenan Yıldız
data.description: All eyes will be on Guler but Yildiz could end up stealing the show this summer with his raw power, skill and speed set to trouble USA, Australia and Paraguay. Alessandro Del Piero, whose famous tongue-out celebration Yildiz has adopted, has been full of praise. "He has a personality that defies his age," Del Piero said. "When I see him play, I see a bit of myself, but he has his own unique magic." The 21-year-old is in red hot form heading into the World Cup, having tormented defenders both domestically and internationally in 2025-26. Make no mistake, Yildiz has just as much potential as Guler to shine this summer. Born in Germany to a Turkish father and German mother, he made his international debut for Turkey – and scored – against Germany in Berlin during a 3-2 win. One of his parents was at the game. “My dad was there, which meant a lot to me," he told Fifa.com. "My mother couldn't make it because we have a small dog and she had to stay at home, so she was watching on TV. I think they were both very proud of me.”

data.name: Altay Bayındır
data.description: Bayindir started his career off as a striker. “I grew up playing on the streets and I used to play as a striker but then I would go in goal to protect our lead after racking up the score because I was the tallest kid,” he has said. Played for Bursaspor and Ankaragucu during his youth career before four years at Fenerbahce. In 2023 he joined Manchester United but playing time has been limited. First he was behind André Onana in the pecking order and then, having started the 2025-26 campaign as the first-choice, he was quickly replaced by Senne Lammens. Bayindir is an agile goalkeeper but he is also prone to the odd mistake. The former United goalkeeper Edwin van der Sar was one of Bayindir’s idols growing up and told the official club website that his family was so happy that he had joined the Premier League side: “We are talking every day, they are incredibly happy for me and I know I have a big responsibility representing the club.”

data.name: Eren Elmali
data.description: Life was good for Elmali in 2025. In February he had joined Galatasaray and by May he had picked up his first Super Lig winner’s medal. In November, however, his world came crashing down as he was suspended for 45 days by the Turkish FA after a widespread investigation into illegal betting on football in the country. He was also removed from the national-team squad that was due to play Spain. He said he had put a bet on five years ago on a game that did not involve his own team at the time, Kasimpasa. He said: "I didn't know I would become this kind of football player back then,” and credits his older brother for intervening before the habit escalated: "My brother told me it was forbidden for a professional [to bet on football games]; he saved my career before it really began." He has since been welcomed back to the fold and earned the trust of Vincenzo Montella.

data.name: Abdülkerim Bardakçı
data.description: A late bloomer who has become the bedrock of the Turkey defence, it is hard to believe he spent years in the footballing wilderness before his ascent on to the main stage. After many years of him flitting between clubs such as Konyaspor, Samsunspor and Giresunspor, Galatasaray gambled on him in 2022 – and have not regretted it. He is now the vice-captain at the club; dominant in the air and surprisingly adept on the ball for such a burly player. Having been part of the team that reached the quarter-finals of Euro 2024, he is going into the World Cup with high hopes. “There is nothing we cannot achieve with this team,” he says. “We have become like a family.”

data.name: Ozan Kabak
data.description: "I've learned something from every league I've played in," says Kabak and it is that breadth of knowledge that makes the central defender such a vital asset for the Turkey coach, Vincenzo Montella. Having joined Galatasaray at a young age, he moved to Germany and Stuttgart at the age of 18 in 2019. He has also played in the Premier League for Liverpool and in the Championship with Norwich. After initially struggling under Jürgen Klopp during a loan spell in 2021 during an injury crisis, he became a fan favourite and even had a song dedicated to him. Sung to the tune of the video game Tetris it went like this: “He’s Ozan. He’s Ozan. He’s Ozan Kabak. Ozan Kabak. Well, he plays centre-back. Well, he plays centre-back for Liverpool.” He speaks four languages and has filled in as the team's unofficial translator. On the pitch he brings tactical intelligence to the defensive unit and is known for his physical bravery and ability to read the game.

data.name: İsmail Yüksek
data.description: Not long ago Yuksek was playing semi-professional football in the Turkish third tier with Golcukspor, acting as a shield for his defence. "I will ensure the most talented players don't have to worry about the ball," he said. He is happy to do the dirty work so that his more technically gifted teammates in attack have more time and space to work their magic. Joined Fenerbahce in 2020 but had to go on several loans before establishing himself in the Super Lig side’s engine room. For the national team he has also become an important player alongside Hakan Calhanoglu in central midfield. In 2024, when asked if he would consider a move abroad, he said: “The important thing is the right time, right conditions, and the system they play in. My adaptation process there is also very important. The club I would like to go to in Europe is my childhood dream, Manchester United.”

data.name: İrfan Can Kahveci
data.description: To salvage his World Cup hopes, Kahveci made a high-profile loan move to Kasimpasa in January, where he has recaptured his form. Despite his fiery temper, he remains a technically gifted player and, listen to this, is one of only three players in history – alongside Ronaldo (R9) and Gareth Bale – to score a Champions League hat-trick and still lose the match. He celebrates his goals with a coffee-drinking gesture and his surname does actually mean coffee maker. In November he was thrown out of the Fenerbahce squad together with Cenk Tosun and then took a break from social media. After a short silence he returned with the following message: “I will write my own thoughts and feelings I believe you [the fans] deserve more than this. During this process, I was completely away from social media and focused on my injury and my family. First of all, I completely respect the decision made. I have no problem with either our management or my coach, and I have not had any throughout this process. The same goes for all my teammates.”

data.name: Mert Müldür
data.description: Born in Vienna to Turkish parents, he made his way through the Rapid Vienna youth teams before making his first-team debut as a 19-year-old in 2018. He had started out as a striker but one day his under-14s coach moved all the players around for a single training session and Muldur ended up as centre-back. He excelled – and never looked back. He speaks five languages: Turkish and German from home, English from school, and Bosnian and Serbian to communicate with his long-term partner. Before Turkey played Austria at Euro 2024 he said: “I grew up in Austria. I went to school in Austria. I learned to play football there and of course there’s a specific connection to Austria. But in Turkey I went through the under-17, under-19 national teams and then the senior national team. And, basically, you don’t have another chance to say yes. So for me, it wasn’t an option to go to Austria.” Having overcome a horrific ankle injury that threatened to derail his career while with Sassuolo in Italy, he has reinvented himself at Fenerbahce.

data.name: Yunus Akgün
data.description: A tireless winger with electrifying pace, he has been a favorite of the Turkey head coach, Vincenzo Montella, since their time together at Adana Demirspor. He is not a starter but is a player Montella trusts. Having initially joined Galatasaray from Adana in 2021 he went out on loan – twice, the second time to Leicester – before establishing himself in the GS first team. While in England his manager, Enzo Maresca, said: “Yunus is struggling a little bit with the rhythm, probably because he said in Turkey the rhythm is a little bit low. Here it’s more quick, the pace of the game. I like Yunus, I would like to give him more minutes, but he needs to adjust and understand how English football is, that it’s more physical.” Had 26 goal involvements in 2024-25 and is not far off those numbers this campaign at the time of writing.

data.name: Ferdi Kadıoğlu
data.description: One of Brighton’s players of the season, Kadioglu has excelled in the Premier League after his move from Fenerbahce in 2024. His manager, Fabian Hürzeler, recently described him as the "ultimate total footballer", noting that his "understanding of the game is so elite that he makes every tactical shift look seamless”. Born in Arnhem to a Turkish father and a Dutch-Canadian mother, he could have represented any of the three countries. After becoming the youngest ever player to represent NEC Nijmegen in the Eredivisie at the age of 16 years and 326 days in 2016, he moved to Fenerbahce two years later. “It was a big step, coming from a small city in Holland to move to Istanbul,” he told the Athletic. “It was huge for me and it was not easy; a new team, a new style of play. Everything was new, so I needed time to adapt.” For the national team he has built a strong connection with Kenan Yildiz down the flank and the duo are a nightmare for the opposition when they get going.

data.name: Barış Alper Yılmaz
data.description: They call him the Tank and he works tirelessly down the wing. He is strong, fast and skilful for such a physical player. He traces his raw power back to his roots in Rize and his father’s trade. "My father was a butcher," he once said. "I grew up eating a lot of meat because of him; and I believe that is where my strength comes from." He may lack the finesse of some of the more technical players in the team, and that can frustrate fans at times, but at his best he is pretty much unplayable. Along with Yildiz he adds physicality to a side that has lacked in this department in the past.

data.name: Kaan Ayhan
data.description: Ayhan can operate at centre-back, right-back or defensive midfield and his utility is one of the main reasons he is in the squad. He is famously close with his family and when he signed for Galatasaray in 2023 he admitted that he still consults his father on almost every major career move. "My father has always been my most honest critic," he said. His tactical intelligence allows him to move between positions and Vincenzo Montella once remarked that he “understands the game better than most coaches". The 31-year-old rarely starts for Turkey but he does offer Montella flexibility from the bench.

data.name: Uğurcan Çakır
data.description: Cakir had big gloves to fill when he moved to Galatasaray in a record-shattering €27.5m deal in 2025, replacing the legendary Fernando Muslera, but he has stepped up to another level and arrives at the World Cup in the form of his life after an impressive season in the league and the Champions League. Born in Antalya, he started his professional career with his boyhood club, Trabzonspor, and made nearly 300 appearances for the club before last year’s switch to GS. The highlight? Winning the Super Lig in 2021-22. “Being part of a team that won the league for the first time in 38 years, contributing towards that triumph and being captain of the team was very special,” he told Unfiltered in 2024. “I will never forget that feeling for the rest of my life.” Representing Turkey means everything to the 30-year-old. "The flag is my motivation; the jersey is my armour,” he has said.

data.name: Oğuz Aydın
data.description: The winger was born in the Netherlands, like Ferdi Kadioglu and Orkun Kokcu. After a productive stint at Alanyaspor, he earned a move to Fenerbahce in 2024. His development took another step forward while playing under José Mourinho. "He always told me to take it ‘step by step’,” he said of the Portuguese. “I kept myself ready and was waiting for my chance. I believe I made the most of it.” Direct, two-footed, and devoid of ego, Aydin is a reliable squad player the Turkey head coach, Vincenzo Montella, can call on when required. He can play across the forward line and is happy being a utility man.

data.name: Samet Akaydin
data.description: His rise to prominence was so unlikely that, when he finally made it to the big time with Adana Demirspor, he admitted that he had kept his old kits from the lower leagues as a reminder of the struggle. "I never forget where I came from; those pitches in the third tier made me the defender I am today," he says. Now 32, he is at Caykur Rizespor after spells with big clubs such as Panathinaikos and Fenerbahce. He remains a polarising figure amid the fan base because of his occasional lapses of concentration, but his physical edge has seen him called up to the national team regularly by the coach, Vincenzo Montella.

data.name: Can Uzun
data.description: Another member of the 2005 golden generation, Uzun has the potential to join Guler and Yildiz as another genuine star of this team. In 2025-26 he showed glimpses of his talent but the campaign was also restricted by injuries and he has yet to establish himself as a starter for the national team. He was born in Regensburg and the German FA was keen to convince him to play for the Nationalmannschaft, but Uzun opted to represent Turkey. "I listened to my heart," he said of his decision to wear the red shirt of the Crescent-Stars. The national-team coach, Vincenzo Montella, is a big admirer, saying: "Our belief in his potential is immense." A silky, versatile attacker who can play as a false 9 or a traditional No 10, drifting between lines, he made his international debut while playing in the German second division with Nürnberg. Keep an eye on him – he could be the surprise package of this team at the World Cup.
